title: Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis
oneLiner: Prevent DVT/PE in hospitalised at-risk patients (surgical, medical, trauma) using mechanical and pharmacological measures; failure kills.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** You are reviewing a 72-year-old man admitted 4 days ago after a right hip fracture repair. He is bed-bound, has a history of moderate obesity and hypertension, and was started on enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily. On your ward round, the nurse mentions he complained of sudden-onset dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain an hour ago. His respiratory rate is 28, SpO₂ 88% on room air, and he appears diaphoretic. You immediately think of pulmonary embolism despite prophylaxis.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
- **Post-surgical hip fracture + immobilisation** → high-risk for VTE (surgery + immobility + age)
- **On enoxaparin prophylaxis** → but still at risk, especially if dose adjusted incorrectly or started late
- **Sudden dyspnoea + pleuritic chest pain** → classic PE presentation, often missed in post-op patients
- **Tachypnoea, low SpO₂, diaphoresis** → signs of right heart strain from acute PE
- **No leg symptoms mentioned** → PE can occur without detectable DVT; don't rely on leg exam

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Subacute dyspnoea:** Elderly patient with gradual worsening oxygen requirement over days, no pleuritic pain → misattributed to pneumonia or heart failure
- **Syncope with hypotension:** Massive PE presenting as vasovagal collapse, especially post-op – think PE even without pleuritic pain
- **Isolated tachycardia:** Post-surgical patient with resting HR 110-120, no obvious cause – may be early sign of small PE; escalate
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis is a bundle of interventions – mechanical (compression devices, stockings) and pharmacological (anticoagulants) – aimed at preventing deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and subsequent pulmonary embolism (PE) in hospitalised patients who are at elevated risk due to immobility, surgery, or medical illness.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** VTE is a leading cause of preventable in-hospital mortality; ~50-60% of surgical patients develop DVT without prophylaxis; prophylaxis reduces risk by >60%.
- **Stakes:** Missed prophylaxis or incorrect dosing can result in fatal PE – a common medicolegal claim. Junior doctors often fail to reassess risk daily or miss drug–drug interactions.
- **Common pitfalls:** Under-dosing in obese patients, forgetting to restart after bleeding episodes, omitting mechanical prophylaxis when pharmacological is contraindicated, and discharging without bridging plan.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of thrombus formation:** Virchow’s triad – stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury. In hospitalised patients:
- **Stasis:** Immobility (bed rest, post-op, stroke) slows venous return in lower limbs, especially in soleal sinuses; blood pools, allowing coagulation factors to accumulate.
- **Hypercoagulability:** Surgery, trauma, malignancy, sepsis activate clotting cascade; acute-phase reactants (fibrinogen, factor VIII) rise; antithrombin levels drop.
- **Endothelial injury:** Surgery, central lines, trauma damage vessel wall, expose subendothelial collagen → platelet activation and thrombus formation.

**Why clinical signs occur:**
- **Asymptomatic DVT** → silent; often first sign is PE. Calf thrombosis can propagate to popliteal/femoral → proximal DVT → higher PE risk.
- **Leg swelling, warmth, pain (DVT)** → inflammation and venous congestion; but many DVT are non-occlusive and asymptomatic.
- **PE signs (dyspnoea, tachypnoea, pleuritic pain)** → thrombus lodges in pulmonary arteries → increased dead space, V/Q mismatch, release of inflammatory mediators → bronchoconstriction, increased pulmonary vascular resistance → right heart strain.
- **Syncope/hypotension with massive PE** → acute right ventricular failure (RV unable to generate enough pressure to push blood against obstructed pulmonary circulation); reduced left ventricular preload → shock.

**Connection to pattern cues:** Post-operative immobility + hypercoagulable state → stasis in leg veins → silent DVT → embolus travels to lungs → sudden dyspnoea. Prophylaxis reduces but does not eliminate risk; especially if delayed or under-dosed.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Surgery (most common)** | Hip/knee arthroplasty, major trauma, abdominal/pelvic cancer surgery | "Post-operative day 2-5" |
| **Medical illness** | Acute MI, heart failure, stroke, sepsis, respiratory failure, inflammatory bowel disease | "Admitted with exacerbation of COPD, now immobile" |
| **Malignancy** | Pancreatic, lung, brain, ovarian cancer; myeloproliferative neoplasms | "New diagnosis of cancer, on chemotherapy" |
| **Acute trauma** | Fractures (lower limb/pelvis), spinal cord injury, prolonged tourniquet | "Polytrauma after RTC" |
| **Immobilisation** | Bed rest >3 days, paralysis, plaster cast, long-haul travel (>4 hours) | "Prolonged immobilisation after fall" |
| **Hypercoagulable states** | Factor V Leiden, antiphospholipid syndrome, pregnancy/postpartum, OCP, HRT | "Young woman with recurrent DVT, family history" |
| **Intravascular devices** | Central venous catheters, PICC lines | "Line-associated DVT" |
| **Dehydration** | Post-op, reduced oral intake, diuretics | "Hypovolaemia and haemoconcentration" |

**Most common cause:** Major orthopaedic surgery (hip/knee) and major abdominal/pelvic surgery. In medical patients, heart failure and respiratory failure are top.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Risk assessment tools (not diagnostic, but guide prophylaxis decisions):**
- **Caprini score** (surgical): risk factor points → low (0-1), moderate (2), high (3-4), highest (≥5)
- **PADUA score** (medical): age≥70, active cancer, previous VTE, reduced mobility, known thrombophilic state → ≥4 = high risk
- **NICE guidelines (UK):** use risk assessment algorithm for all adult admissions; document within 24 hours

**Key tests for VTE (if suspected):**
- **D-dimer** – highly sensitive but not specific; elevated in many conditions (surgery, infection, pregnancy). High negative predictive value – a normal D-dimer in low/moderate risk makes PE unlikely (PERC rule). Do NOT use in high-risk patients (direct to imaging).
- **CUS (compression ultrasound)** – proximal DVT: non-compressible vein confirms DVT; limited for distal calf DVT (low clinical significance). If negative but high suspicion, repeat in 7 days.
- **CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA)** – gold standard for PE; filling defect in pulmonary artery. Avoid in young women or if contrast allergy/renal impairment – consider V/Q scan.
- **V/Q scan** – for patients with contraindication to CT contrast; requires healthy lungs (no COPD, asthma exacerbation). Report as low, intermediate, high probability.
- **Troponin, BNP** – elevated in massive PE with right heart strain; not diagnostic but prognostic.
- **ECG** – may show S1Q3T3, right axis deviation, T-wave inversion in V1-V4, or new atrial fibrillation; but can be normal. Not diagnostic alone.
- **Arterial blood gas** – low PaO₂ with normal PaCO₂ (acute PE → respiratory alkalosis + hypoxia).

**Pattern recognition:**
- **Single finding that confirms PE:** Filling defect on CTPA. **Rules out:** Normal CTPA or normal V/Q scan.
- **Single finding that confirms DVT:** Non-compressible proximal vein on CUS.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – sususpect acute VTE on prophylaxis:**
- **Stop anticoagulation?** No, but hold if next dose due and you suspect PE – no evidence to give extra. Start therapeutic anticoagulation (LMWH, fondaparinux, or oral directly) after diagnosis confirmed.
- **For prophylaxis, not acute treatment:** Prophylaxis is a *preventive* strategy. If PE develops, it's acute VTE management – see separate topic.
- **For suspected haemodynamically unstable PE** – massive PE (systolic BP <90 for 15 min, or requiring inotropes, or cardiac arrest) – call cardiac arrest team / thrombolysis pathway. Give unfractionated heparin bolus (e.g., 5000 IU IV) while awaiting CT.

**Ward (24-72h) – standard prophylaxis protocols:**
- **Pharmacological:** Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) – e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (or 30 mg BD in NICE for certain high-risk groups like hip/knee arthroplasty). Alternative: dalteparin 5000 IU SC daily, tinzaparin 4500 IU SC daily. Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC daily (synthetic factor Xa inhibitor) for patients with HIT.
- **Mechanical:** Intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) devices – inflating cuffs on legs; plus graduated compression stockings (GCS) – avoid in peripheral arterial disease or severe neuropathy.
- **Duration:** For surgery – continue until patient mobilising; for hip/knee – 5 weeks; for cancer surgery – 4 weeks; for medical – until discharge or recovery of mobility (usual 7-14 days).

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not give LMWH if platelet count <50, active bleeding, or recent haemorrhagic stroke.
- Do not use stockings if patient has severe leg oedema, dermatitis, or suspected DVT (swelling may hide DVT, and stockings can cause venous stasis in limbs with acute DVT).
- Do not omit prophylaxis for minor procedures like short-duration surgery (if risk is low, still consider mechanical or low-dose LMWH).

**Long-term:**
- **Discharge planning:** If still at risk (e.g., cancer, immobility at home, previous VTE) – consider extended prophylaxis with LMWH or DOAC (e.g., rivaroxaban 10 mg daily, apixaban 2.5 mg BD). Follow local guidelines.
- **Patient education:** Signs of DVT/PE to report. Encourage early mobilisation.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | **VTE Prophylaxis Failure / VTE** | **Pneumonia** | **Post-op atelectasis** | **Acute MI (STEMI/NSTEMI)** |
|---------|---------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Pain** | Pleuritic, sharp; may be absent | Pleuritic or dull; localised | Usually none; heavy feeling | Crushing, central, radiating to arm/jaw |
| **Exam** | Tachypnoea, signs of DVT (swollen leg) | Fever, crackles, bronchial breathing | Diminished breath sounds, no fever; often afebrile | Diaphoresis, hypotension, S₃ gallop |
| **ECG** | S1Q3T3, RBBB, TWI V1-V4; may be normal | Sinus tachycardia | Usually normal | ST elevation, depression, Q waves |
| **Labs** | D-dimer ↑, ABG: ↓PaO₂, ↓PaCO₂ | WBC ↑, CRP ↑, sputum culture (+), procalcitonin ↑ | Normal; may have mild ↓SpO₂ | Troponin ↑, CK-MB ↑ |
| **Key diff** | CTPA → filling defect; ABG: low PaCO₂ (resp alkalosis) | CXR – consolidation; ABG: low PaO₂ with high PaCO₂? (type 1 or 2 failure) | CXR – linear atelectasis; improves with physio | ECG + troponin; usually no pleuritic pain |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Complication 1: Deep Vein Thrombosis (if prophylaxis omitted or inadequate)**
- **Presentation:** Unilateral leg swelling, warmth, erythema, Homan's sign (dorsiflexion pain – unreliable). But 50% are asymptomatic.
- **Key signs:** Difference in calf circumference >3 cm between legs. Hard, tender cord along vein.
- **What to do:** Urgent CUS. Start therapeutic LMWH + warfarin or DOAC. Do not massage leg (dislodge thrombus).

**Complication 2: Pulmonary Embolism (the life-threatening outcome)**
- **Presentation:** Sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, syncope, circulatory collapse.
- **Key signs:** Tachypnoea, tachycardia, low SpO₂, raised JVP (RV failure). Massive PE – hypotension, shock.
- **What to do:** Urgent CT pulmonary angiogram. Start therapeutic LMWH immediately if high clinical suspicion (avoid delay). In massive PE with shock – call senior for thrombolysis (alteplase 10 mg IV bolus, then 90 mg over 2 h) or surgical embolectomy.

**Complication 3: Bleeding from prophylaxis (e.g., LMWH)**
- **Presentation:** Haematoma at injection site, epistaxis, melaena, haematuria, or intracranial bleed.
- **Key signs:** Drop in Hb, new neurological deficit, hypotension. Check platelets, APTT, anti-Xa if available.
- **What to do:** Stop LMWH, give protamine sulfate (1 mg per 100 IU enoxaparin within 8 hours of last dose). Consider reversal with fresh frozen plasma or recombinant factor VIIa (rarely needed). Then reassess VTE risk vs bleeding risk.

**Late-presentation patterns:** Patient discharged on no prophylaxis after hip replacement returns 3 weeks later with progressive dyspnoea and pleuritic back pain → missed sublethal PE → now chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) – irreversible. Always ensure extended prophylaxis for high-risk procedures.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for risk factors (CAPRINI):**  
C – Cancer (active)  
A – Age >60  
P – Personal/family history of VTE  
R – Recent surgery (within 6 weeks)  
I – Immobilisation / bed rest >72 h  
N – Not walking: paralysis, plaster cast  
I – inflammatory bowel disease, infection, obesity  

**Mnemonic for PE signs (SI Q III T III):** S wave in I, Q wave in III, inverted T in III – not sensitive, but specific if present.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **"Post-op day 2-4 dyspnoea"** – always consider PE before pneumonia or atelectasis.
2. **"Unilateral leg swelling after hip surgery"** – DVT until proven otherwise. Do not rely on absence of Homan's sign.
3. **"Prophylaxis failure does not mean wrong meds"** – check dose adjustment (e.g., enoxaparin 30 mg BD for BMI >40? Often missed). Also check timing – first dose given 6-12h after surgery, not too early (bleeding) or too late.
4. **"Beware of the NPO patient"** – dehydration increases haematocrit and viscosity → add IV fluids as part of prophylaxis bundle.
5. **"Never use aspirin alone for VTE prophylaxis in orthopaedic surgery"** – unless contraindicated to LMWH (NICE does recommend as an alternative, but LMWH is superior).
6. **"Calf thrombosis: proximal extension is the risk"** – if a distal DVT (calf) is identified on ultrasound, repeat scan in 7 days to check for propagation; may need full anticoagulation if high risk.
7. **"Discharge summar is high-risk moment"** – ensure clear plan for extended prophylaxis, especially after hip/knee replacement, cancer surgery, or medical VTE risk (e.g., heart failure with limited mobility). Document duration and follow-up.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Forgetting to reassess risk daily (VTE risk changes with mobility status, new lines, new medications).
- Omitting prophylaxis for patients on antiplatelets (e.g., aspirin, clopidogrel) – the risk of VTE often exceeds bleeding risk; use mechanical + pharmacological if no major contraindication.
- Delaying first dose of LMWH >24 hours after surgery (increases risk without benefit).
- Not adjusting dose for renal impairment (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg daily may need reduction if eGFR <30; consider unfractionated heparin with APTT monitoring).
- Using stockings incorrectly – too tight (risk of necrosis) or wrong size (no effect). Prescribe measured thigh-high stockings unless contraindicated.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **Suspected massive PE with shock:** systolic BP <90 for >15 minutes, altered mental state, or cardiac arrest – call crash team and senior intensivist immediately for thrombolysis pathway.
- **Cardiac arrest** – manage as PEA (pulseless electrical activity) – follow ALS, consider thrombolysis during CPR if massive PE suspected (no CPR of >15 min).
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (or any single parameter red: RR >30, SpO₂ <85%, new confusion) – escalate to critical care outreach team.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** with suspected PE – indicates global hypoperfusion; discuss with senior for urgent CTPA and possible ICU admission.
- **GCS ≤13** or new focal neurology – could be intracranial bleed from anticoagulation (LMWH); stop all anticoagulants and request urgent CT head, discuss with neurosurgery.
- **Haemoglobin drop >20 g/L** with signs of active bleeding (melena, haematemesis, haematuria) – call senior, consider stop anticoag, give reversal agents, arrange urgent blood transfusion and source control.

**For prophylaxis-related decisions (not acute):**  
- If unsure about dose adjustment (e.g., extreme obesity, renal failure) – discuss with haematology pharmacist or thrombosis team.  
- If patient develops heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) – stop LMWH, start fondaparinux or argatroban, consult haematology.  
- If prophylaxis is contraindicated due to bleeding risk (e.g., recent haemorrhagic stroke, active GI bleed) – consider mechanical prophylaxis only and reassess daily.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:** You are reviewing a 72-year-old man admitted 4 days ago after a right hip fracture repair. ### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:** Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis is a bundle of interventions – mechanical (compression devices, stockings) and pharmacological (anticoagulants) – aimed at preventing deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and subsequent pulmonary embolism (PE) in hospitalised patients who are at elevated risk due to immobility, surgery, or medical illness.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** VTE is a leading cause of preventable in-hospital mortality; ~50-60% of surgical patients develop DVT without prophylaxis; prophylaxis reduces risk by >60%.
- **Stakes:** Missed prophylaxis or incorrect dosing can result in fatal PE – a common medicolegal claim. Junior doctors often fail to reassess risk daily or miss drug–drug interactions.
- **Common pitfalls:** Under-dosing in obese patients, forgetting to restart after bleeding episodes, omitting mechanical prophylaxis when pharmacological is contraindicated, and discharging without bridging plan.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The story of thrombus formation:** Virchow’s triad – stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury. In hospitalised patients:
- **Stasis:** Immobility (bed rest, post-op, stroke) slows venous return in lower limbs, especially in soleal sinuses; blood pools, allowing coagulation factors to accumulate.
- **Hypercoagulability:** Surgery, trauma, malignancy, sepsis activate clotting cascade; acute-phase reactants (fibrinogen, factor VIII) rise; antithrombin levels drop.
- **Endothelial injury:** Surgery, central lines, trauma damage vessel wall, expose subendothelial collagen → platelet activation and thrombus formation.

**Why clinical signs occur:**
- **Asymptomatic DVT** → silent; often first sign is PE. Calf thrombosis can propagate to popliteal/femoral → proximal DVT → higher PE risk.
- **Leg swelling, warmth, pain (DVT)** → inflammation and venous congestion; but many DVT are non-occlusive and asymptomatic.
- **PE signs (dyspnoea, tachypnoea, pleuritic pain)** → thrombus lodges in pulmonary arteries → increased dead space, V/Q mismatch, release of inflammatory mediators → bronchoconstriction, increased pulmonary vascular resistance → right heart strain.
- **Syncope/hypotension with massive PE** → acute right ventricular failure (RV unable to generate enough pressure to push blood against obstructed pulmonary circulation); reduced left ventricular preload → shock.

**Connection to pattern cues:** Post-operative immobility + hypercoagulable state → stasis in leg veins → silent DVT → embolus travels to lungs → sudden dyspnoea. Prophylaxis reduces but does not eliminate risk; especially if delayed or under-dosed.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Surgery (most common)** | Hip/knee arthroplasty, major trauma, abdominal/pelvic cancer surgery | "Post-operative day 2-5" |
| **Medical illness** | Acute MI, heart failure, stroke, sepsis, respiratory failure, inflammatory bowel disease | "Admitted with exacerbation of COPD, now immobile" |

| **Intravascular devices** | Central venous catheters, PICC lines | "Line-associated DVT" |

**Most common cause:** Major orthopaedic surgery (hip/knee) and major abdominal/pelvic surgery. In medical patients, heart failure and respiratory failure are top.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Risk assessment tools (not diagnostic, but guide prophylaxis decisions):**
- **Caprini score** (surgical): risk factor points → low (0-1), moderate (2), high (3-4), highest (≥5)
- **PADUA score** (medical): age≥70, active cancer, previous VTE, reduced mobility, known thrombophilic state → ≥4 = high risk
- **NICE guidelines (UK):** use risk assessment algorithm for all adult admissions; document within 24 hours

**Key tests for VTE (if suspected):**
- **D-dimer** – highly sensitive but not specific; elevated in many conditions (surgery, infection, pregnancy). High negative predictive value – a normal D-dimer in low/moderate risk makes PE unlikely (PERC rule). Do NOT use in high-risk patients (direct to imaging).
- **CUS (compression ultrasound)** – proximal DVT: non-compressible vein confirms DVT; limited for distal calf DVT (low clinical significance). If negative but high suspicion, repeat in 7 days.
- **CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA)** – gold standard for PE; filling defect in pulmonary artery. Avoid in young women or if contrast allergy/renal impairment – consider V/Q scan.
- **V/Q scan** – for patients with contraindication to CT contrast; requires healthy lungs (no COPD, asthma exacerbation). Report as low, intermediate, high probability.

**Immediate (first hour) – sususpect acute VTE on prophylaxis:**
- **Stop anticoagulation?** No, but hold if next dose due and you suspect PE – no evidence to give extra. Start therapeutic anticoagulation (LMWH, fondaparinux, or oral directly) after diagnosis confirmed.
- **For prophylaxis, not acute treatment:** Prophylaxis is a *preventive* strategy. If PE develops, it's acute VTE management – see separate topic.

- **Pharmacological:** Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) – e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (or 30 mg BD in NICE for certain high-risk groups like hip/knee arthroplasty). Alternative: dalteparin 5000 IU SC daily, tinzaparin 4500 IU SC daily. Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC daily (synthetic factor Xa inhibitor) for patients with HIT.
- **Mechanical:** Intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) devices – inflating cuffs on legs; plus graduated compression stockings (GCS) – avoid in peripheral arterial disease or severe neuropathy.
- **Duration:** For surgery – continue until patient mobilising; for hip/knee – 5 weeks; for cancer surgery – 4 weeks; for medical – until discharge or recovery of mobility (usual 7-14 days).

- Do not omit prophylaxis for minor procedures like short-duration surgery (if risk is low, still consider mechanical or low-dose LMWH).

**Long-term:**
- **Discharge planning:** If still at risk (e.g., cancer, immobility at home, previous VTE) – consider extended prophylaxis with LMWH or DOAC (e.g., rivaroxaban 10 mg daily, apixaban 2.5 mg BD). Follow local guidelines.
- **Patient education:** Signs of DVT/PE to report. Encourage early mobilisation.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | **VTE Prophylaxis Failure / VTE** | **Pneumonia** | **Post-op atelectasis** | **Acute MI (STEMI/NSTEMI)** |
|---------|---------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Pain** | Pleuritic, sharp; may be absent | Pleuritic or dull; localised | Usually none; heavy feeling | Crushing, central, radiating to arm/jaw |
| **Exam** | Tachypnoea, signs of DVT (swollen leg) | Fever, crackles, bronchial breathing | Diminished breath sounds, no fever; often afebrile | Diaphoresis, hypotension, S₃ gallop |
| **ECG** | S1Q3T3, RBBB, TWI V1-V4; may be normal | Sinus tachycardia | Usually normal | ST elevation, depression, Q waves |
| **Labs** | D-dimer ↑, ABG: ↓PaO₂, ↓PaCO₂ | WBC ↑, CRP ↑, sputum culture (+), procalcitonin ↑ | Normal; may have mild ↓SpO₂ | Troponin ↑, CK-MB ↑ |
| **Key diff** | CTPA → filling defect; ABG: low PaCO₂ (resp alkalosis) | CXR – consolidation; ABG: low PaO₂ with high PaCO₂? (type 1 or 2 failure) | CXR – linear atelectasis; improves with physio | ECG + troponin; usually no pleuritic pain |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**Complication 1: Deep Vein Thrombosis (if prophylaxis omitted or inadequate)**
- **Presentation:** Unilateral leg swelling, warmth, erythema, Homan's sign (dorsiflexion pain – unreliable). But 50% are asymptomatic.
- **Key signs:** Difference in calf circumference >3 cm between legs. Hard, tender cord along vein.
- **What to do:** Urgent CUS. Start therapeutic LMWH + warfarin or DOAC. Do not massage leg (dislodge thrombus).

**Complication 2: Pulmonary Embolism (the life-threatening outcome)**

**Late-presentation patterns:** Patient discharged on no prophylaxis after hip replacement returns 3 weeks later with progressive dyspnoea and pleuritic back pain → missed sublethal PE → now chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) – irreversible. Always ensure extended prophylaxis for high-risk procedures.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonic for risk factors (CAPRINI):**  
C – Cancer (active)  
A – Age >60  
P – Personal/family history of VTE  
R – Recent surgery (within 6 weeks)  
I – Immobilisation / bed rest >72 h  
N – Not walking: paralysis, plaster cast

**Mnemonic for PE signs (SI Q III T III):** S wave in I, Q wave in III, inverted T in III – not sensitive, but specific if present.

2. **"Unilateral leg swelling after hip surgery"** – DVT until proven otherwise. Do not rely on absence of Homan's sign.
3. **"Prophylaxis failure does not mean wrong meds"** – check dose adjustment (e.g., enoxaparin 30 mg BD for BMI >40? Often missed). Also check timing – first dose given 6-12h after surgery, not too early (bleeding) or too late.
4. **"Beware of the NPO patient"** – dehydration increases haematocrit and viscosity → add IV fluids as part of prophylaxis bundle.
5. **"Never use aspirin alone for VTE prophylaxis in orthopaedic surgery"** – unless contraindicated to LMWH (NICE does recommend as an alternative, but LMWH is superior).
6. **"Calf thrombosis: proximal extension is the risk"** – if a distal DVT (calf) is identified on ultrasound, repeat scan in 7 days to check for propagation; may need full anticoagulation if high risk.
7. **"Discharge summar is high-risk moment"** – ensure clear plan for extended prophylaxis, especially after hip/knee replacement, cancer surgery, or medical VTE risk (e.g., heart failure with limited mobility). Document duration and follow-up.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Forgetting to reassess risk daily (VTE risk changes with mobility status, new lines, new medications).
- Omitting prophylaxis for patients on antiplatelets (e.g., aspirin, clopidogrel) – the risk of VTE often exceeds bleeding risk; use mechanical + pharmacological if no major contraindication.
- Delaying first dose of LMWH >24 hours after surgery (increases risk without benefit).
- Not adjusting dose for renal impairment (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg daily may need reduction if eGFR <30; consider unfractionated heparin with APTT monitoring).
- Using stockings incorrectly – too tight (risk of necrosis) or wrong size (no effect). Prescribe measured thigh-high stockings unless contraindicated.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **Suspected massive PE with shock:** systolic BP <90 for >15 minutes, altered mental state, or cardiac arrest – call crash team and senior intensivist immediately for thrombolysis pathway.
- **Cardiac arrest** – manage as PEA (pulseless electrical activity) – follow ALS, consider thrombolysis during CPR if massive PE suspected (no CPR of >15 min).
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** (or any single parameter red: RR >30, SpO₂ <85%, new confusion) – escalate to critical care outreach team.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** with suspected PE – indicates global hypoperfusion; discuss with senior for urgent CTPA and possible ICU admission.
- **GCS ≤13** or new focal neurology – could be intracranial bleed from anticoagulation (LMWH); stop all anticoagulants and request urgent CT head, discuss with neurosurgery.
- **Haemoglobin drop >20 g/L** with signs of active bleeding (melena, haematemesis, haematuria) – call senior, consider stop anticoag, give reversal agents, arrange urgent blood transfusion and source control.

**For prophylaxis-related decisions (not acute):**  
- If unsure about dose adjustment (e.g., extreme obesity, renal failure) – discuss with haematology pharmacist or thrombosis team.  
- If patient develops heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) – stop LMWH, start fondaparinux or argatroban, consult haematology.  
- If prophylaxis is contraindicated due to bleeding risk (e.g., recent haemorrhagic stroke, active GI bleed) – consider mechanical prophylaxis only and reassess daily.
